step.title: Step 20
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract LEFT x10 to release Green')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 92, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blue stuck at cols 24-27, retract not releasing it. Moving agent after grabbing may have broken the release mechanism. Need new strategy.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Try extending RIGHT again to grab a different block (Green), which might release Blue. First move DOWN to align with Green.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 91 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After extending RIGHT, Green moved from cols 18-21 to 24-27. So now both Blue and Green are at cols 24-27. Blue is at rows 19-22, Green is at rows 31-34.
So extending RIGHT grabbed Green (moved it to cols 24-27), but Blue is still at cols 24-27 from before. This means I now have two blocks grabbed (Blue and Green), both at cols 24-27.
This is getting messy. Let me think about what the goal really is.
Looking at the bottom panel: Pink (agent), Red, Green, Blue. This shows the order Red → Green → Blue.
Currently, the blocks are arranged as:
- Red: 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: 19-22, cols 24-27
- Green: 31-34, cols 24-27
The order by row is Red, Blue, Green. But the target is Red, Green, Blue.
I need to swap Blue and Green. But my current approach of grabbing and moving isn't working well because:
1. Once I grab a block and move the agent, the block gets stuck and can't be released.
2. Grabbing multiple blocks just moves them all to the same column.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the goal isn't to rearrange the blocks physically, but to connect them in the correct order using the path.
Looking at the bottom panel again: Pink, Red, Green, Blue. This might mean I need to extend the path to Red first, then Green, then Blue.
But I've already connected to all blocks. The issue might be the order of connection.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the initial state. The blocks were originally at:
- Red: 19-22
- Blue: 25-28
- Green: 31-34
And the target order is Red, Green, Blue. So the blocks need to be rearranged from Red-Blue-Green to Red-Green-Blue.
My earlier observation was that when I grab a block and move the agent, the unheld blocks move with the agent. This is the key mechanic for rearranging.
But the issue is that once I move the agent after grabbing, the held block gets stuck and can't be released.
Let me try a different approach: grab a block, DON'T move the agent, then retract. This should release the block at its original position.
Actually, that doesn't help because I need to move the agent to rearrange the blocks.
Wait, let me re-examine the earlier successful case:
- Initial: Red 19-22, Blue 25-28, Green 31-34
- Grab Green (extend RIGHT): Green at cols 42-45
- Move UP: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green stays at 31-34
- Retract LEFT x10: Green at cols 18-21
In this case, the retract worked! Green was released at cols 18-21.
So the retract DOES work, but only if I retract enough. Let me check how many LEFT actions I need.
In the successful case, I retracted LEFT x10. In the failed cases, I retracted LEFT x5 or LEFT x3.
Maybe I need to retract more LEFT actions to fully release the block.
Current state:
- Agent: 30-35
- Red: 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: 19-22, cols 24-27
- Green: 31-34, cols 24-27 (grabbed)
Let me try retracting LEFT x10 to release Green.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Both Blue and Green now at cols 24-27. Earlier successful retract used LEFT x10. My recent retracts used only LEFT x3-5 which wasn't enough. Need more LEFT actions to release.
Plan: Retract LEFT x10 to release Green, then try the rearrangement strategy again more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: Agent 30-35, Red 13-16, Blue 19-22 (cols 24-27), Green 31-34 (cols 24-27, grabbed)
# Try retracting LEFT x10 to release Green

predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract LEFT x10 to release Green')
action(['LEFT'] * 10)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After retract x10 ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Check if Green was released (should be at cols 18-21)
# Also check if Blue was released
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== After retract x10 ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 24-27
  Agent: rows 30-35
